coredata.dc:title: Amide salt pyrolysis fabrication of graphene nanosheets with multi-excitation single color emission
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hao
Cao, Duxia
Cheng, Xiao
Guan, Ruifang
Zhou, Chuanjian
coredata.dc:description: A novel and simple approach of using amide salt pyrolysis to produce photoluminescent (multi-excitation and single color emission) graphene nanosheets (GNs) with a thickness of <1 nm and a diameter of about 100–200 nm is described herein. It has characteristics of high water solubility, low toxicity, easy manufacturing, etc., and has potential application prospects in analytical chemistry and biomedicine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amide salt
Multi-excitation
Single color emission
Graphene nanosheets
ClO− probe
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of ClO– By a fluorescence method
Practical application for detection of ClO–

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, (b) AFM, (c) XRD, (d) Raman, (e) FT-IR and (f) XPS survey spectrum of GNs, and high-resolution XPS of (g) C1s, (h) N1s, and (i) O1s core level spectra of GNs.
The absorption spectra (a), the best excitation spectra (b) and the best emission spectra (c) of GNs aqueous solution at different concentrations.

(a) Excitation and emission spectra of GNs, (b) the CIE color coordinates of the GNs. (c), (d), and (e) are the emission spectra obtained in different excitation wavelength ranges (240 nm–260 nm; 280 nm–320 nm; 340 nm–560 nm). (f) 3D scan of GNs. (The concentration of GNs aqueous solution is 0.1 mg/mL).

(a) and (b) The excitation spectra and emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of GNs aqueous solution. (c) The emission spectrum obtained by excitation at 300 nm is divided into two peaks (λem1 = 485 nm, λem2 = 404 nm).
(a), (b), and (c) are the emission spectra obtained before and after adding ClO− at different excitation wavelengths.
Mechanism of multi-excitation and single color emission of GNs.

(a) The timedependent fluorescence intensity curve after adding ClO− to GNs, (b) the fluorescence intensity of GNs at different pH values with and without ClO−, (c) the fluorescence spectra of GNs in the presence of different ions (500.0 μM), (d) the fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of ClO−in the GNs solution, (e) the semilogarithmic correlations between the ClO− concentrations (0–500.0 μM) and the fluorescence intensities of the GNs, (f) linear relationship of Log(I0/I) and the concentration (0–35.0 μM) of ClO−, (g) absorption spectrum of ClO−, and the absorption spectra before and after GNs combined with ClO−, (h) the fluorescence lifetime spectra before and after GNs combined with ClO−, (i) FTIR spectra before and after GNs combined with ClO−.
(a–c) Fluorescence microscopy images of HepG2 cells, and (d–f) microscopy images of HepG2 cells further treated with NaClO for 10 min.
Synthesis of amide salt and GNs.

head.ce:title: Amide salt pyrolysis fabrication of graphene nanosheets with multi-excitation single color emission

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Institute of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Institute of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan 250061, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel and simple approach of using amide salt pyrolysis to produce photoluminescent (multi-excitation and single color emission) graphene nanosheets (GNs) with a thickness of <1 nm and a diameter of about 100–200 nm is described herein. It has characteristics of high water solubility, low toxicity, easy manufacturing, etc., and has potential application prospects in analytical chemistry and biomedicine.

ce:section.ce:para: As a two-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with unique optical, physical, and chemical properties, graphene nanosheets (GNs) have attracted much attention due to their advanced applications in semiconductor devices, biomedicine, photoelectric catalysis, and chemical sensing [1–7]. The synthesis strategies of GNs are “top-down” and “bottom-up,” including the reduction of graphene oxide [8], epitaxial growth on silicon carbide [9], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [10,11], exfoliation of graphite crystals [12], and microwave-assisted synthesis [13]. Presently, the Hummers method or other improved Hummers method is still used mostly to oxidize the graphite powder for GN chemical preparation [14,15]. In contrast, it is still difficult to prepare graphene nanosheets through the “bottom-up” method using small organic molecules as raw materials. Moreover, these current methods involve expensive equipments, complicated and cumbersome synthesis steps, and the generation of toxic wastes.

The fluorescence sensor based on GNs has attracted broad interest because of its simple operation, fast response, high sensitivity and real-time monitoring. However, graphene itself is a zero bandgap semiconductor, and it does not have photoluminescence properties. Many studies have shown that edges, stacking, lateral dimensions, defects, and quantum confinement effects significantly affect the optoelectronic properties of GNs [16–19]. Therefore, its optical performance can be changed by adjusting its energy band structure, including cutting two-dimensional GNs into zero-dimensional quantum dots [20], destroying the overall π system through physical and chemical modification or oxidation treatment [21], and adopting hybrid atoms into the raw materials through the “bottom-up” approach [22].

In this work, a rapid and straightforward method for synthesizing fluorescent GNs by amide salt pyrolysis was proposed for the first time. This method has several apparent advantages: novelty, rapidness, toxic solvent exclusion, and large scale preparation. It is also unique as the GNs synthesized in this study have the properties of multiple excitations (three excitation bands) and single color emission. Furthermore, the luminescence mechanism of the GNs has been determined by characterizing and analyzing the structure and fluorescence properties of GNs. Additionally, GNs have excellent water solubility, low toxicity, and can sensitively, efficiently, and specifically, respond to ClO− and image HepG2 cells.

Citric acid, N-(2-aminoethyl)morpholine and inorganic salts were purchased from Aladdin Reagents and used without further purification. Nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (NMR, MercuryPlus-400), Raman spectra (XploRA), X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS; ESCALAB 250XI), X-ray diffraction (XRD; X'Pert PRO MPD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100plus), atomic force microscopy (AFM; Bruker Dimension ICON), Fourier-transform infrared spectra (FT-IR, NICOLET-iS50), UV–vis absorption spectra (Shimadzu, UV2550), and fluorescence spectra (Horiba, Fluoromax-4) were used to characterize the obtained products.

Synthesis of ammonium salt: 1.92 g (0.01 moL) citric acid was dissolved in 20 mL absolute ethanol, and 3.91 g (0.03 moL) N-(2-aminoethyl)morpholine was dissolved in 40 mL absolute ethanol. Then, the ethanol solution of N-(2-aminoethyl)morpholine was slowly and dropwisely added to the ethanol solution of citric acid under an ice-bath, and stirred for 8 h to make the reaction complete. Finally, petroleum ether was added to the mixed solution to precipitate a white solid, and a white powdery amide salt was obtained after rotary evaporation.

GNs synthesis: The amide salt powder was placed in a crucible and placed in a tube furnace, and then heated to 300 °C at a heating rate of 2 °C/min in a nitrogen atmosphere, and the holding time was 0.5 h, which yielded a black, bulky solid. Then, it was ground in an agate mortar and sonicated in deionized water for 0.5 h, followed by filtering the undissolved part using a 0.22 μM filter membrane, finally, the GNs were obtained after freeze–drying the dissolved part (Scheme 1 ).

All fluorescence measurements were conducted at room temperature. Sodium hydroxide (0.1 M) and hydrochloric acid (0.1 M) were used to adjust the pH of the GN aqueous solution. In the ion detection analysis, all anions and cations derived from sodium salt and hydrochloride (except Ag+, AgNO3), the excitation wavelength was set to 375 nm, and the excitation and emission slit width was 5 nm.

HepG2 cells were used to test the cytotoxicity of GNs by the MTT method, and they were cultured according to the reference method in cell imaging experiments [23,24]. Fluorescence cell images were obtained by Nikon A1MP confocal microscopy.

The structure and morphology of the GNs were first confirmed by various characterization methods. TEM shows that the carbon nanomaterial has a circular structure with a diameter of about 100 nm and is well dispersed in an aqueous solution (Fig. 1
a). After increasing the magnification further, it was revealed that these GNs show lattice fringes with a pitch of 0.34 nm (Fig. 1 a inset), corresponding to the graphitic carbon (002) diffraction surface [25]. The AFM results show that most of the GNs are nanosheets with a diameter of about 100–200 nm, and their height is about 0.9 nm (Fig. 1 b), which is the typical height of graphene. TEM (Fig. 1 a) and AFM (Fig. 1 b) results indicate that well-crystallized GNs were successfully synthesized. The XRD spectrum of GNs shows a relatively broad peak near 19.8° (Fig. 1 c), which is smaller than that of graphite. This indicates that there are defects (organic functional groups) in the carbon core of GNs, making the interlayer spacing greater than that of graphite. The Raman spectrum of GNs shows two broad peaks at 1367 and 1580 cm−1 (Fig. 1 d), corresponding to the “disorder” D band and crystalline G band, respectively. The ratio of D to G band intensity (ID/IG) is 0.89, indicating that GNs are composed of a nanocrystalline core of sp2 carbon atoms of graphite, but there are still many defects of sp3 carbon on the edges and surfaces. Fig. 1 e shows the FT-IR spectrum of GNs. The broad peaks at 3300–3500 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of NH and OH. The peaks near 2950, 2856, and 2812 cm−1 can be assigned to the bending vibration of CH, and the peaks at 1778 and 1700 cm−1 are attributed to the tensile vibration of CO and CC, respectively. Such a large CC tensile vibration peak proves the high degree of carbonization of GNs. Additionally, the small peaks at 1650 and 1580 cm−1 derived from the vibration modes I and II of the amide group, which indicates that the amide salt undergoes dehydration and condensation at high temperatures to form an amide bond. Finally, the peaks at 1400 and 1115 cm−1 are caused by the tensile vibrations of CN heterocycles and COC (epoxy), respectively. FT-IR results show that GNs are not completely carbonized, and there are several oxygen-containing functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, amide, and epoxy) in their structure, which makes them more water-soluble. Next, the structure of GNs was further analyzed by XPS (Fig. 1 f–i). GNs mainly contain three elements: carbon (70.05%), nitrogen (14.12%), and oxygen (15.83%). The high-resolution C 1s spectrum can be divided into four peaks, which can be assigned to CC/CC (284.43 eV), CN (285.23 eV), CO (286.18 eV), and CN/CO (287.43 eV). In the high-resolution N 1s spectrum, three peaks are located at 398.48 eV, 399.48 eV, and 400.53 eV, corresponding to the nitrogen atoms of pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite nitrogen, respectively. The O 1s XPS spectrum can be divided into two peaks, pointing to CO (531.43 eV) and C–OH/CO (530.43 eV). This result is consistent with the FT-IR result. Based on the above analysis, it can be concluded that GNs have been successfully synthesized. Additionally, the as prepared GNs are composed of a nanocrystalline core of sp2 carbon atoms of graphite, and there are many defects of sp3 carbon on the edges and surfaces.

Surprisingly, GNs exhibit multiple excitation peaks and single color emission properties, which are rare in fluorescent carbon materials and arouse our intense curiosity. First, the fluorescence emission mechanism and photophysical properties of GN aqueous solution were studied by ultraviolet absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2
a, three bands can be clearly seen in the absorption spectrum of GN aqueous solution. It shows an absorption band within 200–260 nm, and the absorption band is red-shifted with increasing concentration. It is designated as the π–π* transition of the carbon core (CC, sp2), which is called “core state” [26,27]. In the excitation spectrum (Fig. 2 b), there is a more obvious excitation peak (the first excitation peak) in the range of 230–270 nm, which corresponds to the absorption band of the “core state.” However, as the concentration increases, its intensity first increases and then decreases significantly until disappears completely. In a concentrated solution, excessive aggregation of fluorophores prevents the excitation of the GN core [28]. Second, it shows a relatively gentle absorption band within 260 ∼ 320 nm, but there is a very strong excitation peak (the second excitation peak) in the same wavelength range of the excitation spectrum (Fig. 2 b). This absorption band is designated as the n–σ* transition generated by isolated organic functional groups (hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, epoxy, amide, etc.) containing lone pairs of electrons in the plane and edge of the GN carbon core [28]. Additionally, in the excitation spectrum, as the concentration of GN aqueous solution increases, the intensity of the excitation peak at 260–320 nm first increases and then decreases. This is because the number of isolated functional groups gradually increases to a maximum with the increase of the GN concentration [28,29]. Then, the isolated functional groups gradually decreases due to the rise in the degree of aggregation. Finally, the absorption band at 340–600 nm is designated as the n–π* transition caused by the aggregation of organic functional groups at the edge of GNs, which is called the “edge state” [26,27]. Interestingly, the absorption peak intensity of this band gradually increases with the increase in concentration, and the wavelength range gradually expands to the long-wavelength region. The corresponding optimal excitation peak (the third excitation peak) and optimal emission peak (excitation at the best excitation wavelength (n–π* transition band)) intensities gradually increase and have a significant red-shift, showing a concentration dependence (Fig. 2 b and c). The reason is that as concentration increases, the organic functional groups at the edges of the GNs will produce stronger aggregations, thereby enhancing the fluorescence and reducing the bandgap. This property can be explained by observing the photos of GNs aqueous solution of different concentrations under 365 nm UV light ( Fig. S2 ). Additionally, it can be found that the difference between the excitation peak near 400 nm and that near 300 nm gradually increases as the concentration increases. The reason is that the functional groups on the surface and edges of GNs are more likely to aggregate in concentrated solutions, so the aggregated fluorescent groups are much more than the separated functional groups. Additionally, the photoluminescence quantum yield of as-prepared GNs is as high as 11.2%, which is significantly higher than that reported previously [30].

Studies have shown that NaBH4 can selectively reduce carbonyl and epoxy groups to hydroxyl groups [31–33]. As shown in Fig. S3 , the intensity of the n–π* transition absorption peak and the third excitation peak of GNs did not change after adding NaBH4, indicating that there are no carbonyl and epoxy groups in the edge state. The slight increase in the intensity of the absorption peak and the second excitation peak of the n–σ* transition may be due to defects, such as carbonyl and epoxy groups, in the GN structure, which are reduced to hydroxyl groups. The intensity of the π–π* transition absorption peak and the first excitation peak increased significantly. The reason is that the reduction of in-plane defects of GNs and the increase of hydroxyl groups as electron donors strengthen the π-conjugated system of GNs. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of GN aqueous solutions at different concentrations was also studied ( Fig. S4 ), and the results showed that the measured average lifetime remained unchanged, indicating that the luminescence center did not change. Moreover, the decrease in the intensity of the excitation peak and emission peak at higher concentrations (1.6 mg/mL) may be caused by the internal filtration effect (Fig. 2 c) [34].

Next, the emission spectra of GN aqueous solutions of different concentrations under different excitation wavelengths are studied in detail. As is shown in Fig. 3
a, there are three obvious excitation peaks in the excitation spectrum of the dilute solution (Fig. 3 a). Three emission peaks are obtained when excited with three optimal excitation wavelengths, which are (λex1 = 248 nm, λem = 464 nm), (λex2 = 309 nm, λem = 482 nm) and (λex3 = 375 nm, λem = 468 nm), but the CIE color coordinates of GNs are all located in the blue region (Fig. 3 b), showing the ability of multiple excitation and single color emission. Additionally, the emission wavelengths were found to exhibit different regularities when excited in the wavelength range corresponding to the three different excitation peaks. When the excitation wavelength is in the range of 240–260 nm and 280–320 nm, the best emission wavelength is stabilized at 464 nm and 482 nm, respectively, and both exhibit emissions irrelevant to excitation (Fig. 3 c–d). However, when the excitation wavelength is increased from 340 to 560 nm, it shows an apparent red-shift behavior (Fig. 3 e), which may be caused by the difference in the edge states of single GNs. These photophysical properties can be observed in more detail in the 3D scanning spectra of GNs (Fig. 3 f). Additionally, the emission spectra of GN aqueous solution of a higher concentration and GN ethanol solution of different concentrations under different excitation wavelengths were studied by the same characterization method, which can be further understood in Figs. S5, S6, S7 , and Table S1.

The multi-excitation and single color emission phenomena of GNs are novel and fascinating, which inspires our interest in in-depth exploration. After carefully observing the emission spectra of the GN aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths, the existence of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process can be found. When excited at 240–260 nm (Fig. 4
a), the emission peak near 470 nm partially overlaps with the third excitation peak. Additionally, there is a small fluorescence peak near 305 nm in the emission spectrum, and also a large overlap between the position of the small fluorescence peak and the second excitation peak. Similarly, when excited at 280–320 nm (Fig. 4 b), the emission spectrum of GNs overlaps with the third excitation peak, and a smaller bump appears near 400 nm. After the peak-splitting process (Fig. 4 c), two fluorescence peaks centered at 404 and 485 nm can be seen. Additionally, GN ethanol solution of different concentrations have the same phenomenon and are more obvious ( Fig. S7 ). The above characteristics can be considered as evidence of the existence of FRET [29]. First, there are several overlaps between the emission and excitation spectra of GNs. Second, the emission centers of three electronic transitions (π–π*, n–σ*, and n–π*) are closely connected in the GN structure, and the distance is close enough to meet the space distance condition. Also, the appearance of these small fluorescence peaks is because some of the emission centers do not meet the distance condition or the spectra are not entirely overlapped.

In the experiment, it was found that ClO− can significantly quench the fluorescence emission of GNs. Interestingly, evidence of the existence of FRET was also found while analyzing the response of GNs to ClO−. It can be clearly observed from Fig. 5 that when excited at the third excitation wavelength (λex3 = 375 nm), the fluorescence emission intensity is significantly reduced, but the optimal emission wavelength remains unchanged (Fig. 5 a). When excited at the second (λex2 = 309 nm, Fig. 5 b) or the first excitation wavelength (λex1 = 248 nm, Fig. 5 c), the fluorescence emission intensity near 480 nm is significantly reduced or even disappears, and a relatively weak fluorescence peak appears near 400 nm. By carefully observing the absorption spectrum of Fig. 7 g, it can be found that the addition of ClO− makes the absorption peak of GN change significantly, especially the intensity of the absorption peak of the n–π* transition. Therefore, it is believed that the appearance of the weak fluorescence peak near 400 nm is because the energy absorbed by the GNs at the lower wavelength cannot be transferred with the n–π* transition absorption peak.

The above analysis shows the mechanism of multi-excitation and single color emission of the GNs as shown in Fig. 6
. UV light at 248 nm is used to excite the π–π* electronic transition in the core state, and UV light at 309 nm causes the isolated chromophore to produce n–σ* electronic transitions. The n–π* electronic transition is caused by the edge states excited by 375 nm UV light. In this process, the energy absorbed by the core state of GNs is quickly transferred to the isolated chromophore and the edge state gathering place through the FRET process. Moreover, the isolated chromophore itself can also absorb energy and be transfer to the edge state gathering place. Finally, as the electrons at the gathering place of GNs drop from the excited state to the ground state, blue light is gradually emitted.

Fluorescence stability and antiinterference ability are essential for practical applications. Next, the effects of photobleaching time, pH, and the ion concentration of NaCl on the fluorescence properties of GNs were studied. As shown in Fig. S8
a, the fluorescence intensity of GNs decreased by 18% after 12 h of irradiation under a 365 nm UV lamp, but with the extension of time, the fluorescence intensity was relatively stable and remained above 80%. The results show that GNs have better resistance to photobleaching than other fluorescent carbon materials. Fig. S8
b shows that the fluorescence intensity of GNs at different concentrations of NaCl solution (0–1 mol/L) remains almost unchanged, offering excellent fluorescence stability. Fig. S8
c shows the fluorescence intensity of GNs at different pH (λex3 = 375 nm; λem = 468 nm). Interestingly, there is a clear “volcano” shape between the pH and the fluorescence intensity of GNs, and the activity reaches the highest level at pH 3.5. The significant decrease in fluorescence intensity at higher and lower pH may be caused by the protonation or deprotonation of the edgestate organic functional groups of GNs [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of ClO– By a fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorite has high antibacterial effects and oxidizing properties, so it is widely used as a disinfectant and bleaching agent in daily life [35]. However, due to improper operation by humans, high concentrations of ClO− are widespread in the environment, which seriously affects the lives and health of humans, animals, and plants. Excessive ClO− can cause various diseases, including lung injury, cardiovascular disease, cancer, renal disease, and rheumatoid arthritis [35–39]. Most current detection methods require expensive instruments and cumbersome operating procedures, and do not have realtime monitoring capabilities. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop an environmentfriendly, highly selective, sensitive, reliable, and realtime monitoring method to detect ClO– in the environment. According to literature reports [31,34,40], many fluorescent carbon materials have been extensively studied and used to detect ClO− in the environment, but most of their behavior in response to ClO− is not specific. Some metal ions, such as Cu2+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Al3+ and Zn2+, can also quench their fluorescence emission. Therefore, when there are multiple response ions in the environment, the detection process will be complicated. Fortunately, in this study, GNs can respond to ClO− and are highly specific. This may be because GNs have a higher preparation temperature, which is much higher than the preparation temperature of general fluorescent carbon dots and polymer dots [25,34,41–44], which makes them contain very few functional groups such as amino nitrogen that can be combined with metal ions, so they cannot respond to metal ions. To the best of our knowledge, there are almost no reports of fluorescent carbon materials that can specifically respond to ClO−.

GNs have excellent water solubility and fluorescence stability, which shows their potential application value in environmental science. To improve the performance of GNs in detecting ClO−, the response time and response pH were optimized before conducting detailed studies. It can be seen from Fig. 7 a that the fluorescence intensity of the GN aqueous solution dropped sharply after adding ClO− and remained in a relatively stable state within 1 min. So, 1 min was chosen as the best response time. Fig. 7 b shows the fluorescence intensity values in the absence and presence of ClO− in GN aqueous solutions at different pH. There is the greatest difference at pH 3.5. At lower pH, the protonation of GNs makes the fluorescence intensity drop significantly, which is not conducive to fluorescence detection. At higher pH, the deprotonation of GNs reduces the fluorescence, and the oxidation ability of ClO– is also significantly reduced, which in turn weakens the fluorescence quenching effect [31]. Therefore, to make the detection effect more sensitive, pH 3.5 is selected as the pH value of the detection solution.

Fluorescent materials must have excellent selectivity and antiinterference ability in practical applications. Selectivity can be evaluated from Fig. 7c, only ClO− can significantly quench the fluorescence emission of GN aqueous solution, and other common metal cations, anions, and oxidants have no effect on the fluorescence intensity of GN aqueous solution. Additionally, the presence of these interfering substances will not reduce the ClO−response effect of GNs ( Fig. S9 ). Some oxidants (such as potassium permanganate) may have a more substantial oxidizing effect than ClO−, but they are not common in actual water environments. The sensitivity can be evaluated from Fig. 7 d–f, the fluorescence intensity of GNs gradually decreases as the concentration of ClO− in the solution increases. When the concentration increased to 50.0 μM, the fluorescence intensity of GNs quickly decreased to 1/10 of the original. However, when the concentration of ClO− continues to increase, its fluorescence reduction effect begins to decrease significantly. Surprisingly, there is an excellent semilogarithic correlation between the fluorescence quenching efficiency of GNs and the concentration of ClO− (0.01–35.0 μM) (Fig. 7 f), and it satisfies the linear equation Log(I0/I) = 0.02452C–0.01237 (R2 = 0.9989, n = 8), where I0 and I are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of ClO−, respectively, and C represents the concentration of ClO−. The calculated detection limit is 11.2 nM [42], far lower than that of other fluorescent carbon materials and organic dyes (Table S2). Such excellent selectivity, sensitivity, and antiinterference ability mean that GNs have the expected potential to be applied to ClO– detection in the environment.

To understand the mechanism of GNs in response to ClO−, the changes in absorption, fluorescence lifetime, and infrared spectra of GNs before and after the addition of ClO−were studied. It can be seen from the absorption spectrum of Fig. 7 g that ClO− has an absorption peak near 295 nm, while the absorption peaks belonging to ClO− and GNs almost completely disappear after ClO− is added to the GN solution, and the fluorescence lifetime is also reduced from 4.80 ns to 2.37 ns (Fig. 7 h). Simultaneously, when the three best excitation wavelengths were used to excite GNs, their fluorescence emission peaks were significantly reduced. As we all know, ClO−has a powerful oxidizing ability, and the hydroxyl group on the GN structure is a reducing functional group, which easily interacts with oxidizing substances to quench its fluorescence emission. Therefore, fluorescence detection mechanism may be related to the hydroxyl group in the structure of GNs. From the FTIR spectrum of Fig. 7 i, it can be seen that the stretching vibration peak (3300–3500 cm−1) of the hydroxyl group of GNs is significantly reduced after the addition of ClO−. Additionally, when NaBH4 was continuously added to the GN solution quenched by ClO−, the fluorescence emission of GNs was restored to a certain extent ( Fig. S10 ). The reason is that NaBH4 reduces that part of the oxidized hydroxyl group. As a result, it is confirmed that ClO− quenches the fluorescence emission by oxidizing the hydroxyl of GNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application for detection of ClO–
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the application of GNs in real water samples, tap water from the laboratory of the University of Jinan was collected and used for testing without any pretreatment. It was found that the concentration of ClO− in the original water sample was 3.4 μM. A series of water samples with different concentrations of ClO− were analyzed, and the test results are summarized in Table S3. It can be seen that the recoveries of all analyzed samples are in the range of 98.0%–103.0%, and the relative standard deviation is less than 2.5% (n = 5). The results show that GNs can detect ClO− in actual environmental water samples.

To understand the potential application of GNs in the biological field, the cytotoxicity of GNs was evaluated by the MTT method. As shown in Fig. S11 , when the concentration of GNs in HepG2 cells is as high as 120 μg/mL, the average cell survival rate is still greater than 83%. The results indicate that the GNs as probes have low cytotoxicity and can be used for images in living cells. For cell imaging tests, the concentration of GNs is 50 μg/mL. After incubating HepG2 cells with GN solution at 37 °C for 2 h, bright fluorescence was observed in the cytoplasm of HepG2 through a confocal fluorescence microscope (Fig. 8 ), proving its potential for application in the field of cell biology. To further demonstrate the practical applicability of the chemosensor GNs for NaClO in biological samples, the GNs was preliminarily applied to fluorescence imaging of intracellular NaClO in living cells. By contrast, after incubation with 20 μM NaClO for 10 min further, the fluorescence intensity in channel decreased significantly (Fig. 8). These results indicated that the GNs was able to detect endogenous ClO− in living cells.

In summary, we developed a new strategy to prepare GNs with a diameter of about 100–200 nm and thickness less than 1 nm using amide salts as precursors for pyrolysis in a tube furnace. The synthesized GNs have multiexcitation and single color emission properties due to the energy transfer of different emission centers. The research results also show that GNs have excellent water solubility, fluorescence stability, and low toxicity; thus, can sensitively, efficiently, quickly, and precisely respond to ClO− in the environment and image HepG2 cells.

Hao Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Duxia Cao: Investigation, Data curation. Xiao Cheng: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ruifang Guan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Chuanjian Zhou: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21477047, 51903139) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MEM006).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.07.009.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable Synthesis and Assembly of BiomassDerived B/N CoDoped Carbon Nanosheets with Ultrahigh Aspect Ratio for High-Performance Supercapacitors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Ling , Z. Wang , M. Zhang , C. Yu , G. Wang , Y. Dong , S. Liu , Y. Wang , J. Qiu , Sustainable Synthesis and Assembly of BiomassDerived B/N CoDoped Carbon Nanosheets with Ultrahigh Aspect Ratio for High-Performance Supercapacitors , Adv. Funct. Mater. 26 ( 1 ) ( 2016 ) 111 – 119 .

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrastable Surface-Dominated Pseudocapacitive Potassium Storage Enabled by Edge-Enriched NDoped Porous Carbon Nanosheets
